attributes.stem:
A 45-year-old woman presents to the General Practice clinic with worsening shortness of breath and productive cough with brown mucus plugs over three months. She has a history of difficult-to-control asthma since childhood and takes regular fluticasone/salmeterol inhaler and montelukast. She has required three courses of oral prednisolone in the past year for exacerbations. She denies any skin rashes, joint pains, or haemoptysis. She works as a librarian and has no relevant travel history.

On examination, she appears mildly short of breath. Her pulse rate is 88/min, blood pressure 125/75 mmHg, respiratory rate 20/min, SpO2 94% on room air, and temperature 37.0°C. Chest examination reveals widespread wheeze.

Investigations reveal:

  • Haemoglobin: 138 g/L (115-160)

  • White cell count: 11.2 x 10⁹/L (4.0-11.0)

  • Neutrophils: 6.8 x 10⁹/L (2.0-7.5)

  • Eosinophils: 842 cells/µL (0-500)

  • Platelets: 295 x 10⁹/L (150-400)

  • C-reactive protein: 18 mg/L (<5)

  • Total IgE: 2486 IU/mL (0-100)

  • Chest X-ray: Central bronchiectasis with parallel line opacities (tramline shadows) and finger-in-glove opacities in both upper zones
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with features characteristic of allergic bronchopulmonary aspergillosis (ABPA). The combination of poorly controlled asthma, markedly elevated total IgE, peripheral eosinophilia, and characteristic chest X-ray changes strongly supports this diagnosis. The presence of brown mucus plugs is particularly suggestive.

Allergic bronchopulmonary aspergillosis is a hypersensitivity reaction to Aspergillus fumigatus colonising the airways. It typically occurs in patients with underlying asthma or cystic fibrosis. The clinical course is characterised by recurrent episodes of bronchial obstruction, inflammation, and mucoid impaction. Patients often experience wheezing, productive cough with thick mucus plugs, and progressive decline in lung function if untreated. Complications can include permanent bronchiectasis, pulmonary fibrosis, and chronic respiratory failure. Treatment typically involves systemic corticosteroids and antifungal medications, with the goal of preventing further lung damage.

Chronic eosinophilic pneumonia can present with similar symptoms and peripheral eosinophilia, but typically shows peripheral opacities on imaging ('photographic negative' of pulmonary oedema), and would not explain the markedly elevated total IgE.

Invasive pulmonary aspergillosis typically occurs in immunocompromised patients and presents with fever, chest pain, and possible hemoptysis. This patient is immunocompetent and lacks these features.

Churg-Strauss syndrome (eosinophilic granulomatosis with polyangiitis) can present with asthma and eosinophilia but typically involves other organ systems such as skin, peripheral nerves, or sinuses, which are absent in this case.

Bronchiectasis with bacterial colonisation would not explain the elevated IgE or eosinophilia. While bronchiectasis is present, the pattern of central bronchiectasis with finger-in-glove opacities is classic for ABPA.
learningPoints: Allergic bronchopulmonary aspergillosis (ABPA) should be suspected in patients with asthma who have deteriorating lung function and elevated total IgE.
Chest X-ray findings in ABPA classically include central bronchiectasis, finger-in-glove opacities, and tramline shadows.
Brown mucus plugs and peripheral eosinophilia are characteristic clinical features of ABPA.
references: Agarwal, Ritesh,; Sehgal, Inderpaul S; Dhooria, Sahajal; Muthu, Valliappan; Prasad, Kuruswamy T.; Bal, Amanjit; Aggarwal, Ashutosh N.; Chakrabarti, Arunaloke. Allergic bronchopulmonary aspergillosis. Indian Journal of Medical Research 151(6):p 529-549, June 2020. | DOI: 10.4103/ijmr.IJMR_1187_19
Greenberger PA, Bush RK, Demain JG, Luong A, Slavin RG, Knutsen AP. Allergic bronchopulmonary aspergillosis. The Journal of Allergy and Clinical Immunology: In Practice. 2014 Nov 1;2(6):703-8.
